In this lesson, we're going to talk about planning out the final stages of your deal. By this time, you have an offer that's been accepted by the seller. We'll cover everything you need to do next to get the deal over the finish line. To kick things off, let's talk about securing deal exclusivity. When you reach the stage of the deal, you'll want to have an exclusive right to move forward with the seller. You can outline this exclusivity in the LOI, which we'll talk about in the next part. During this period of exclusivity, you'll be looking to accomplish three things. Do your due diligence. On your end, you'll want to do due diligence on financial, commercial, legal, other areas and aspects of the business, especially ones that your advisors point you to. You want to finish your formal fundraising. You can work with different lenders to check receivables and associated contracts. These people will help you formalize your due diligence process. You want to hire contingent fee advisor. Now that you have exclusivity and proof of funds, you can hire these advisors and organize your team to take care of everything. Let's talk about the heads of terms. The heads of terms in an LOI can be referred to as the memorandum of understanding or MOU, the memorandum of agreement MOA, or the letter of intent, LOI. These documents outline an agreement between the buyer and the seller. They're mutually accepted expectations. The heads of the terms themselves are not legally binding contracts, but they signal that the binding contract is coming. We've discussed LOIs earlier. An LOI or exclusive LOI could also include heads of terms, or they might be separate documents. This is a very important document in any deal, and you should not be doing any work on the due diligence side until you have one. The heads of terms also gives the lawyers a broad stroke about what's been agreed upon. That way they can move forward in their legal contracts and legal due diligence. It's also where you map out the exclusivity that we talked about a little while ago. You also use the heads of terms to prevent the seller from accelerating receivables, collecting early cash, or liquidating inventory for cash. This is important because your financing might be dependent on the status of these. And when you take over the business, you want the financials to remain in the state that they were when the offer was accepted by the seller. When we outline the heads of terms, we want to make sure we include things that prevent those issues from arising. First, specifically the working capital. You determine working capital by looking at the latest balance sheet. Then on your heads of terms, you would set a target for closing. In outlining this target, you would set a range for the seller to hit, which should be in line with how the business is already operating. And if the business doesn't hit that mark, there are repercussions. This could be the buyer putting more money in the business. It could be a reduction in a purchase price. It's a whole litany of solutions, but you want to call it out at the beginning. Second, you want to make sure that the net current assets are stable. The net current assets are cash, trade receivables, inventories, payables, accruals, short-term bank debt, etc. Like working capital, you want to outline a cause in your heads of terms restricting what the seller and the business can do prior to a sale with these assets. You can set a target based on the business's net current asset number as a broad stroke to prevent extreme changes in those assets. Third, you want to see a minimum level of cash in the business that you should have on hand when you close. Just like the previous two, you want to enforce the business to maintain a certain level of cash on hand. If that number is $200,000, you want the business to maintain this level of cash and have clear repercussions if whatever requirement is not met. So you want to set a dollar amount that the business is not allowed to fall under when the deal closes. You don't want to inherit a business where you don't have adequate amounts of cash on hand to trade within the business. And you want to have a surplus of cash on hand to invest into the business once you do purchase it. Now, let's move on to the project plan. The project plan is where we structure how to execute everything in the heads of terms we just covered. We'll cover all of this stuff on the project plan through closing, where everything goes, who does what, what gets done, and so on. It'll be everything that's essential on the seller and buyer side. From there, your job is to project manage and review the plan frequently and regularly with the seller depending on how hands on you are or you want to be. If you are the commercial due diligence person, you're going to have your own responsibilities within that framework. The project plan is going to have all the people involved in getting the deal done. That will be you, the seller, your lawyer, your accountant, any consultants, the seller's lawyer, accountants, bankers, and so on. And you'll want to have their names, phone numbers, emails, all the different ways to contact them so everyone knows how to communicate. You also want to list any time frames, anyone or everyone will be absent so you can plan for it. Finally, you'll have the physical closing. Everyone involved will come together either at the seller's lawyer's office or at the actual business itself and you'll all sign the documents and finalize everything. If you've ever bought a house, it's a lot like that. You'll see everyone shake hands, then the deal is done. And that's the deal closing plan. I'll see you at the next lesson.
